title: An autoethnographic exploration of music-assisted progressive muscle relaxation
author: De Jager, Caelan
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Across the body of research examined in the following literature  review, the musical stimuli that accompanied each protocol was  preselected the vast majority of the time. This research stems out  of a desire to test not only curated music, but my selection of  music as well, in order to explore which stimuli category I respond  the most favourably to, build an in-depth knowledge of the topic  and reflect over my own experience with the protocol through  thematic analysis and comparison of biofeedback data to my  qualitative experience 
 The following study will serve as an autoethnographic exploration  of my personal experience engaging in a music-assisted  progressive muscle relaxation protocol over a period of 9 days.  Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) was a protocol initially  designed by American physician E. Jacobson (Edmund, 1929) as a  method of deep muscle relaxation which seeks to help reduce  stress and anxiety. Around the same time Jacobson’s  development of PMR was published, post World War 1, so to was  music therapy gaining popularity (Montinari et al., 2018). The first  applications of music-assisted PMR were thought to have arisen  around this time, combining the benefits of both the protocol and  music itself as an effective aid to help reduce stress and anxiety.  The exercise does this by orderly tensing and then relaxing muscle  groups throughout the body to combat muscle tension developed  alongside stressful situations or thoughts (Edmund, 1929).

 The following study will serve as an autoethnographic exploration  of my personal experience engaging in a music-assisted  progressive muscle relaxation protocol over a period of 9 days.  Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) was a protocol initially  designed by American physician E. The first  applications of music-assisted PMR were thought to have arisen  around this time, combining the benefits of both the protocol and  music itself as an effective aid to help reduce stress and anxiety.  The exercise does this by orderly tensing and then relaxing muscle  groups throughout the body to combat muscle tension developed  alongside stressful situations or thoughts (Edmund, 1929).
